title: Liquidity Fluctuations in Over-the-Counter Markets
abstract: This paper proposes a theory of excess price fluctuations in over-the-counter secondary markets. When heterogeneous assets trade under asymmetric information, a quality effect emerges: high liquidity lowers the quality of the pool of sellers and decreases future liquidity. Cyclical equilibria can arise even without fundamental shocks. In a cycle, investors speculate by bidding up the price of low-quality assets, anticipating a high resale price at the peak. When this resale effect is strong, cycles disappear and multiple steady states coexist with different levels of liquidity. The model rationalizes empirical patterns for corporate bonds and housing in particular.

article.introduction:
Asset prices often appear disconnectedfrom fundamentals. This weak relationship shows as excess price fluctuations, documented notably for corporate bonds and housing by Bao and Pan (2013) and Glaeser et al. (2014). Price deviations from fundamentals are sometimes extreme during speculative episodes when prices rapidly increase before crashing (see Cutler, Poterba, and Summers1991). In some asset classes, such as housing, the repeated nature of these episodes is puzzling. Why can price and trading volume fluctuate so much in the absence of changes to fundamentals?
This paper proposes a theory of endogenous liquidity fluctuations that can rationalize these phenomena. Motivated by the markets for corporate bonds and housing, I consider a secondary market in which heterogeneous assets trade under asymmetric information.1Asset owners face idiosyncratic liquidity shocks that generate gains from (re)trade. Although the supply of each asset quality is fixed, I show that the average quality of assets for sale and thus current liquidity are negatively related to past liquidity. This endogenous quality effect can generate cyclical fluctuations in equilibrium. In a cycle, price dynamics are speculative due to a resale effect. If future liquidity is high, forward-looking buyers who expect to easily resell a low-quality asset bid up its price today. This speculative behavior leads to endogenous asset price booms and crashes. When cycles do not exist, these resale considerations instead generate multiple equilibria.
To describe the quality effect and the resale effect, I turn to the model. The framework is stylized but is best applied to opaque over-the-counter (OTC) markets with heterogeneous assets, such as housing or corporate bonds. In the model, investors trade assets available in fixed supply. Each asset is characterized by its quality, called high or low, and each investor holds zero or one unit. Each period, with some probability, an asset owner experiences an idiosyncratic liquidity shock that captures a portfolio rebalancing need. In the model, a liquidity-hit investor has a lower private value from holding the asset. An investor with a low valuation can gain from selling his asset to a buyer with a high valuation. Buyers and sellers meet bilaterally in a decentralized market as in Duffie, Garleanu, and Pedersen (2005). The key friction is that only the current owner knows the quality of his asset and his own private valuation. This learning-by-holding assumption is meant to capture opacity of OTC markets in a tractable way.
In a bilateral OTC meeting, the buyer (she) makes the seller (he) a take-it-or-leave-it offer for the asset. The buyer faces adverse selection because she is uninformed about the asset's quality and the private valuation of the seller. She offers either a high price, or a low price, or she randomizes between both. The high price targets investors who own a high-quality asset and wish to sell because they have a low valuation. Under asymmetric information, owners of low-quality assets can also trade at this price and earn information rents. To avoid paying these rents, the buyer can instead offer the low price, targeted at low-valuation sellers of low-quality assets, but she would forgo the gains from trade with sellers of high-quality assets.
Market liquidity is defined as the (mixing) probability that a buyer makes a high offer. If buyers make a low offer, some gains from trade for high-quality assets are not realized compared to the symmetric information case. Market liquidity increases with the average quality of assets for sale, and decreases with the information rents conceded to low-quality sellers when making a high offer. Both variables are endogenous in a dynamic model: The composition of the pool of sellers responds to past liquidity, and, with forward-looking investors, information rents depend on future liquidity.
The first dynamic force at play in this opaque OTC market is a quality effect. The total supply of each asset quality is fixed but the share held by low-valuation investors is endogenous. This observation is crucial because high-quality assets may only be supplied by low-valuation investors as high-valuation owners cannot gain from trade. Suppose then most owners of high-quality assets have a low valuation. Buyers, who face a favorable seller pool, offer a high price. Next period, high-quality assets are held by these high-valuation buyers who do not sell unless they experience a liquidity shock. Faced with a less favorable pool, new buyers offer a low price: the market becomes illiquid. In an illiquid market, the potential supply of high-quality assets gradually increases as owners experience liquidity shocks but do not sell because buyers offer a low price. Eventually, the potential supply can be high enough for buyers to offer a high price again. The existence of the quality effect hinges only on the assumption that liquidity shocks are persistent.
The forward-looking behavior of investors who can buy and resell assets determines price dynamics in this secondary market. There exists a resale effect in that future market liquidity increases the value of low-quality assets. This value combines the holding value of the asset and the option value to resell it in the future. High future prices raise the resale value of a low-quality asset and thus its current value to a potential buyer, reducing information rents today. This resale effect implies that buyers are more likely to make a high-price offer if future buyers also make a high-price offer. The effect is stronger if investors are more forward-looking as the option to (re)sell is a larger component of the total value.
The interaction between the quality effect and the resale effect determines the liquidity dynamics of opaque secondary markets. With few plausible ingredients, the model can deliver a rich set of stationary equilibria that I fully characterize. A simple stability criterion is used to refine the equilibrium set in the case of multiplicity. The key parameter of interest is the (fixed) fraction of high-quality assets, which proxies for market quality.
The equilibrium is straightforward for extreme values of market quality. If it is very high, buyers always find it optimal to make a high offer despite the quality effect: the equilibrium is a liquid steady state. Similarly, if market quality is poor, the equilibrium is an illiquid steady state in which buyers always make a low offer. More interesting dynamics emerge when market quality is intermediate. The type and properties of the equilibrium then depend on the strength of the quality effect relative to the resale effect.
When the discount factor of investors and the probability of a liquidity shock are low, the quality effect is strong. For intermediate values of market quality, the only stable equilibrium is a cycle with periodT≥2$T\ge 2$.2AT$T$-period cycle consists ofT−1$T-1$trough periods when only low-quality assets trade and one peak period when buyers make a high offer. Trading at the peak clears the market for high-quality assets, leading to the trough. In the trough, the pool quality improves until the next peak, due to the quality effect. While only low-quality assets are traded in the trough, the price increases to reflect the option value to later resell at the peak. Hence, due to the resale effect, price dynamics in opaque OTC markets can be speculative.
The length of the cycle is decreasing with market quality. If market quality is low, it takes longer for the pool to improve enough to justify a high offer by buyers at the peak. The analysis also shows that longer cycles are harder to sustain because the resale effect acts as a countervailing force. In a cycle, buyers must find it optimal to make a high-price offer at the peak. While the pool of sellers is most favorable, information rents are also maximal: The next peak isT$T$periods away, as opposed to one period away before the peak. For a longer cycle, the seller pool improvement is weaker while the negative effect due to the resale effect is stronger. As a result, the more investors are forward-looking, the shorter the equilibrium cycle is.
When investors' discount factor is high, the resale effect is very strong and no cycles, even short ones, exist in equilibrium. Instead, because market liquidity is driven mostly by investors' expectations about future market liquidity, two stable equilibria coexist: a high-price steady state and a low-price steady state. Intuitively, if future buyers make a high offer, current buyers also make a high offer because information rents are low. Conversely, if future buyers make a low offer, current buyers are more inclined to make a low offer. Hence, liquidity is self-fulfilling in opaque secondary markets when resale considerations dominate (see Moore (2013)).3
To assess the cost of liquidity fluctuations in OTC markets, I compare the two-period cycle with the coexisting steady state in which buyers randomize between a high and a low offer. This steady-state equilibrium does not survive the stability analysis, but it provides a clean benchmark for the cyclical equilibrium. Under risk-neutrality, social welfare is maximized when the average misallocation of high-quality assets is minimized. In the two-period cycle, liquidity is high precisely when the effective supply of high-quality assets is high, thereby reducing average misallocation. This result implies that the cycle can dominate the steady state. However, another force plays against cycles when market quality is high. Market liquidity increases with market quality in the steady state, and misallocation decreases. In a cycle, the average probability of a high offer stays constant at12$\frac{1}{2}$and misallocation does not vanish. The increase in market quality translates instead into higher buyer profits at the peak, with no effect on liquidity or welfare.
The model offers stark predictions for price dynamics when the equilibrium is cyclical. Asset prices fluctuate in excess of fundamentals and can be speculative. These predictions are related in the next paragraphs to empirical patterns in two markets motivating this analysis: corporate bonds and housing. Given that the theoretical analysis highlights the importance of the quality effect for cycles, the results are best applied to a pool of similar assets rather than a single asset in isolation. In the corporate bond market, one can interpret the assets as a pool of securities with the same ratings but hard-to-observe variation in credit quality.
The first prediction of the model is that asset prices fluctuate in excess of fundamentals. In the model, equilibrium fluctuations can arise although asset fundamentals are constant. As such, the results provide a liquidity-based rationale for excess price fluctuations in corporate bonds, which is documented by Bao and Pan (2013). That liquidity fluctuations contribute to (excess) price movements in this market is supported empirically by Bai, Bali, and Wen (2019). They show that a corporate bond liquidity factor exhibit strong fluctuations that are not explained by traditional equity factors. In addition, as rising prices ultimately fall in a cycle, the model predicts reversals in corporate bond prices. This result is supported by Bao, Pan, and Wang (2011) and corroborates their interpretation of price reversals as an illiquidity proxy.
The model also provides a tractable theory of speculative behavior with repeated booms and busts. In the trough of a cycle, buyers speculate by acquiring low-quality assets at low prices to later resell them at a high price at the peak. Prices build up and then revert once high-quality assets also trade. Cyclical equilibrium prices thus exhibit all of the features that Cutler, Poterba, and Summers (1991) associate with speculative dynamics identified in many asset classes. The model further predicts that prices accelerate in the boom phase, in line with the characterization of bubbles by Greenwood, Shleifer, and You (2019). Finally, the expected holding time of an asset decreases as the bubble grows, a pattern observed by Zwick, Nathanson, and DeFusco (2020) during the 2000 to 2005 housing boom.
Relation to the Literature. This paper contributes to a large literature on dynamic adverse selection. To the best of my knowledge, it is the first to derive a tractable model of liquidity cycles based on the quality effect. Several studies extend the framework of Akerlof (1970) by letting agents trade over several periods but without retrade (see Janssen and Roy (2002), Camargo and Lester (2014), Fuchs and Skrzypacz (2015), Moreno and Wooders (2016), and Kim (2017)). In these models, time on the market acts as a screening device: lemons trade first, market quality improves, and good assets eventually trade. Zryumov (2015) and Hu (2018) embed these dynamics in a model of entrepreneurial finance with aggregate productivity shocks and entry. In good times, all entrepreneurs enter and immediately receive financing at a pooling rate. In bad times, the screening mechanism described above is at work. My model differs from these papers in two important ways: the transition between regimes is purely endogenous, and the resale effect drives price dynamics.4Relatedly, Eisfeldt (2004), Kurlat (2013), and Bigio (2015) present macro models in which liquidity varies because of aggregate shocks, that is, fluctuations are still fundamental.
My work is closest to Chiu and Koeppl (2016) and Asriyan, Fuchs, and Green (2019). As in my model, investors learn by holding, assets are retraded, and trading history is unobservable. Both of these papers also find that steady states with different degrees of liquidity coexist with a strong resale effect.5Asriyan, Fuchs, and Green (2019) use this multiplicity to build stochastic equilibria driven by sunspots, which I do not consider. Instead, I characterize predictable cycles generated by the quality effect. This effect is not active in Asriyan, Fuchs, and Green (2019), who assume that liquidity shocks are i.i.d. Chiu and Koeppl (2016) also consider persistent shocks, but they do not characterize cycles. In my model, if a cycle exists, which requires parametric restrictions, it is selected by a stability criterion over the steady state.6
Relatedly, Inderst (2005) studies a matching market with adverse selection and entry applied to the labor market. As in this paper, the composition of the pool of sellers/workers is endogenous, but only steady states are considered. Guerrieri and Shimer (2014) and Chang (2018) study an environment similar to mine, but use competitive search instead of random search. Because their equilibrium is typically separating, the composition of the pool of sellers is irrelevant in their model and the resale effect is muted. This paper also relates to the new monetarist literature in which assets are used as a means of exchange (see Lagos, Rocheteau, and Wright (2017) for a survey). Building on Velde, Weber, and Wright (1999), studies like Rocheteau (2011) and Bajaj (2018) consider a setup with asymmetric information about asset quality. In these models, an asset owner is a borrower by construction. In the language of my model, all asset owners are hit by a liquidity shock, a feature that shuts down any quality effect. Interestingly, cycles can still arise in these models but for very different reasons. Rocheteau and Wright (2013) show that a high future asset price relaxes today's credit constraints and thus decreases today's asset liquidity premium, generating endogenous fluctuations in this premium. Several studies analyze government interventions in markets with adverse selection. Philippon and Skreta (2012) or Tirole (2012) consider a static setup, while Fuchs and Skrzypacz (2015) consider a dynamic setup without resale.
The rest of the paper is organized as follows. SectionIdescribes the model. SectionIIanalyzes the case of myopic investors with a zero discount factor. This limit case delivers the key intuition for the existence of cycles based on the quality effect. SectionIIIintroduces the resale effect with forward-looking investors and SectionIVdescribes the equilibrium in this general case. SectionVcontains empirical predictions for the corporate bond market and the housing market. SectionVIstudies the welfare properties of cycles. SectionVIIconcludes. Proofs are in AppendixA.
article.I. The Model:
Time is discrete, indexed byt$t$, and runs forever. The economy is populated by risk-neutral and infinitely lived investors with discount factorδ<1$\delta <1$. There is a fixed supply of heterogeneous assets normalized to one unit with two qualities, (H)igh or (L)ow, with respective sharesq$q$and1−q$1-q$. Throughout the paper, I refer toq$q$as market quality. Each asset is indivisible, and investors may hold either zero or one unit.7Asset holders have either a (h)igh valuation for assets or a (l)ow valuation. Investors who do not own an asset have a high valuation. The utility flow from holding an asset depends on the asset quality and on the private valuation of the owner. These utility flows are denoted byuai$u^{i}_{a}$, wherei∈{l,h}$i\in \lbrace l,h\rbrace$stands for the private valuation anda∈{L,H}$a\in \lbrace L,H\rbrace$for the asset quality. Utility flows are ordered as follows.

For both qualities, there are gains from trade between low-valuation and high-valuation investors. The inequalityuLh<uHl$u_{L}^{h}<u_{H}^{l}$further implies that anH$H$asset is more valuable than anL$L$asset, regardless of the owner's private valuation. This last condition is not crucial for the results, as will become clear. I call a typeia$ia$investor an asset owner with valuationi∈{l,h}$i\in \lbrace l,h\rbrace$and asset qualitya∈{L,H}$a\in \lbrace L,H\rbrace$. Each period, an asset owner with valuationh$h$is hit by a liquidity shock with probabilityγ∈(0,1)$\gamma \in (0,1)$. The endowment allows a buyer to purchase any asset from any owner type.
Assumption 2. (Large Endowment)


The key trading friction is that both the quality of the asset held by an investor and his valuation are the investor's private information.9Trading takes place in a decentralized market with bilateral random matching, the OTC market. Each period, each asset owner is matched with a buyer drawn from a pool of nonasset owners of size larger than one. If trade occurs, the seller leaves the asset market forever, while if trade fails, the buyer leaves the market forever.10InInternet AppendixIA.II, I analyze the case in which asset owners face matching frictions.
In a match, the uninformed buyer (she) makes a take-it-or-leave-it offer for the asset of the informed seller (he).11In this screening game, the buyer assigns beliefμa,ti$\mu ^{i}_{a,t}$that the asset owner has typeia$ia$. Under random matching, the rational beliefμa,ti$\mu ^{i}_{a,t}$is the share of typeia$ia$among asset owners, and I denote byμt={μa,ti}a=L,Hi=l,h$\bm{\mu }_{t}=\lbrace \mu ^{i}_{a,t}\rbrace ^{i=l,h}_{a=L,H}$the distribution across types in periodt$t$. The strategy of a buyer who matches with a seller in periodt$t$is a distributionFt$F_{t}$over prices given beliefsμt$\bm{\mu }_{t}$.12A seller's strategy is an acceptance decision given the price offer. The solution concept for the bilateral trading game is the perfect Bayesian equilibrium.
Discussion of the Assumptions. As will become clear, the results hinge on three key ingredients of the model: an asset quality is only known to the current owner, liquidity shocks that generate trade are persistent, and trading history is unobservable.
Learning by Holding. This simplifying assumption is also made by Chiu and Koeppl (2016) and Asriyan, Fuchs, and Green (2019) to ensure tractability. For real assets such as housing, Martel, Mirkin, and Waters (forthcoming) document price patterns suggestive of learning by holding. Plantin (2009) observes that some financial assets entitle their owner to superior information. According to Ivashina and Sun (2011), syndicated lenders routinely receive private information from corporate borrowers during loan amendments. In a market with heterogeneous assets such as corporate bonds, a buyer may need to evaluate many assets prior to trading. It is therefore plausible that the seller that owns the asset has superior information.
Persistent Liquidity Shocks. Liquidity shocks capture household relocation needs. For financial assets, liquidity shocks can be interpreted as hedging needs due to changes in the valuation of other portfolio assets or new private investment opportunities. With these interpretations in mind, it is reasonable to assume that the liquidity shock persists until the asset is sold. An investor that faces tight funding constraints or concentration limits may be unable to undertake new investments before old positions are closed.
Opacity of Trade History. With observable trade history, a buyer could infer the quality of an asset from its trade history if assets of different quality exhibited different trading patterns. When trade history is opaque, this screening tool is not available. This assumption makes it easier for low-quality asset buyers to resell their assets, and, as I discuss later, is necessary for cycles to emerge.13Empirically, in OTC markets, assets transit through dealers before reaching end customers (see Hollifield, Neklyudov, and Spatt (2017) for asset-backed securities or Li and Schürhoff (2019) for municipal bonds). Hence, trading history is difficult to obtain. The ability of financial intermediaries to pool and tranche securities in “kitchen-sink” bonds or barbell-shaped loan pools also contributes to the opacity of assets' trading history.

article.III. Liquidity Dynamics and Resale Effec:
I now analyze the general case in investors' discount factorδ$\delta$is strictly positive. The key difference relative to SectionIIis that investors' valuation for the asset is now endogenous as it incorporates expectations about future market liquidity. The analysis below will show that equilibrium dynamics are also driven by a resale effect whereby current market liquidity increases with future market liquidity. As will become clear, the resale effect introduces a speculative component to asset prices, and it generates richer dynamics.
To solve for the trading game of SectionIIwithδ=0$\delta =0$, I use the ranking between holding values imposed by Assumption1. The trading game withδ>0$\delta >0$is similar after replacing holding valuesuai$u^{i}_{a}$with endogenous reservation pricesra,ti$r^{i}_{a,t}$. Lemma1states that a seller with a lower holding value also has a lower reservation price. Intuitively, the reservation price has two components: the present value of holding the asset forever and the option value of selling it in some future period. The option value component is the same across all investors because buyers make uninformed offers. However, the holding value component increases with the current utility flow, which explains why the static ranking of types according to utility flows is preserved in the dynamic model. Future liquidity increases current liquidity via two channels, as captured by each term on the right-hand side of equation (16a). The first term is the difference between the high pricerHl$r^{l}_{H}$and a buyer's valuation for anL$L$asset,rL,th$r^{h}_{L,t}$. This pure resale effect lowers information rents today. Intuitively, a buyer is less worried about adverse selection if he can resell a lemon at a high price.
The second term,rL,th−rL,tl$r^{h}_{L,t}-r^{l}_{L,t}$, which enters negatively, is the trading profit from a low offer. This profit decreases with future market liquidityπt+1$\pi _{t+1}$, all else equal, due to an intertemporal competition effect. Ifπt+1=1$\pi _{t+1}=1$, both thelL$lL$asset owner and a potentialh$h$-valuation buyer can then sell at a high price in periodt+1$t+1$. Hence, this intertemporal competition effect also implies that future liquidity begets current liquidity.
The pure resale effect and the intertemporal competition effect induce similar dynamics in liquidity. For simplicity, in what follows I use the term resale effect for both channels. Note that the dynamic competition effect is absent whenuLh=uLl$u^{h}_{L}=u^{l}_{L}$, which is sometimes analyzed as a limit case. This resale effect is strong if investors' discount factorδ$\delta$is high.
The equilibrium analysis in the general case is centered on the long-run dynamics of the model. Whenδ>0$\delta >0$, transition dynamics do not have a Markov structure with respect to the predetermined state variableμHl$\mu ^{l}_{H}$. As discussed above, the solution to the buyer optimization problem also depends on reservation pricesra,ti$r^{i}_{a,t}$, which are not predetermined and may depend on the entire future path of market liquidity{πt+k}k≥1$\lbrace \pi _{t+k}\rbrace _{k\ge 1}$. Hence, the focus is on (Markov) stationary equilibria in which fluctuations are driven only by changes in the predetermined state variableμHl$\mu ^{l}_{H}$.
Definition 2.A stationary equilibrium is given by a periodT$T$, a state spaceM={μH,tl}t=1,..,T$\mathcal {M}=\lbrace \mu _{H,t}^{l}\rbrace _{t=1,..,T}$, a buyer mixing probability functionπ(.)$\pi (.)$, and reservation price functions{rai(.)}a=L,Hi=l,h$\lbrace r_{a}^{i}(.)\rbrace _{a=L,H}^{i=l,h}$defined overM$\mathcal {M}$such that, for anyt∈[|1,T|]$t\in [|1,T|]$, (withT+1$T+1$=1 by convention),

The cardinalityT$T$of the state spaceM$\mathcal {M}$is called the period of the equilibrium.

The first condition of Definition2formalizes the optimal choice of buyers between a high offer and a low offer. The second and third conditions respectively state that the sequence of reservation prices and the law of motion for the asset distribution must be consistent with the buyers' optimal choice to form part of an equilibrium. A stationary equilibrium is characterized by the state space for the effective supply ofH$H$assetsμHl$\mu ^{l}_{H}$. Because there is no aggregate exogenous shock in this model, fluctuations over this state space are purely endogenous if they exist.
A stationary equilibrium is characterized by its period, that is, the smallest time intervalT$T$between two visits of a given valueμHl$\mu ^{l}_{H}$of the state space. I introduce the following taxonomy for stationary equilibria.
Definition 3.A steady state is a stationary equilibrium with periodT=1$T=1$. A cycle is a stationary equilibrium with periodT≥2$T\ge 2$.

In a steady state, endogenous variables are constant over time. In a cycle, however, the economy fluctuates deterministically over a nondegenerate state space. As the analysis will show, there can be several stationary equilibria for some parameter values. To refine the equilibrium set, I use a stability criterion based on the standard concept of Lyapunov stability for discrete-time systems (see, for instance, Anishchenko, Vadivasova, and Strelkova (2014)). The underlying logic is intuitive: a stationary equilibrium is stable if, following any small initial perturbation of the state variable, there is an equilibrium path that converges back to this stationary equilibrium. In the interest of space, the formal definition of this criterion is given in AppendixB.
article.IV. Equilibria with Resale Effect:
I analyze first the weak resale effect case, assuming (C1$C_{1}$) holds.
Proposition 2.There exist market quality thresholds{q̲T,q¯T}T≥1$\lbrace \protect\underline{q}_{T},\bar{q}_{T}\rbrace _{T\ge 1}$and discount factor thresholds{δ¯T}T≥2$\lbrace \bar{\delta }_{T}\rbrace _{T\ge 2}$such that under (C1$C_{1}$), the following hold:


The expressions for thresholdsq̲T$\protect\underline{q}_{T}$,q¯T$\bar{q}_{T}$, andδ¯T$\bar{\delta }_{T}$are given in the proof. Proposition2extends the results of Proposition1to the case in which condition (C1$C_{1}$) holds. When market quality is high (low), the equilibrium is a liquid (illiquid) steady state. When market quality is intermediate, cycles with one peak period and several trough periods emerge, but only ifδ$\delta$is low enough, that is, the resale effect is weak. Proposition2also shows that other equilibria exist when market quality is intermediate, notably a steady state in which buyers mix between offers with a constant probability. As shown below, only the cycle without mixing survives the stability criterion.18
As explained in SectionII, cycles are generated by the quality effect. To avoid repetition, I focus here on the role of the resale effect. The key intuition for existence condition (CT${C_{T}}$) is that the resale effect acts as a countervailing force on the quality effect. While the quality of the seller pool is best (worse) at the peak (trough) of the cycle, resale prospects follow an opposite pattern. Consider a buyer at the peak of aT$T$-period cycle. The market will be illiquid in the nextT−1$T-1$periods, so information rents are highest at the peak. When information rents are high, a low offer is attractive, but the equilibrium play is a high offer. Conversely, in the period before the peak, information rents are lowest because the market is liquid in the next period. This makes it difficult to sustain a low offer in equilibrium before the peak because low information rents increase the attractiveness of a high offer.
Additional results about existence condition (CT${C_{T}}$) can be obtained in the limit case with no gain from trading the low-quality asset.19
Corollary 1.IfuLh=uLl$u^{h}_{L}=u^{l}_{L}$, existence condition (CT${C_{T}}$) for cycles is tightened whenγ$\gamma$increases, that is,δ¯T$\bar{\delta }_{T}$is decreasing inγ$\gamma$.

Corollary1is intuitive. The parameter1−γ$1-\gamma$measures the strength of the quality effect. A strong quality effect can balance a strong resale effect, which is why cycles may exist for high values of the discount factor whenγ$\gamma$is low. Figure2illustrates existence condition (CT${C_{T}}$) for a two-period cycle. The upper limit of the colored region corresponds to the mappingγ↦δ¯2$\gamma \mapsto \bar{\delta }_{2}$. Lighter shades of color correspond to larger intervals[q̲1,q¯1]$[\protect\underline{q}_{1},\bar{q}_{1}]$of market quality where the two-period cycle exists. Figure2suggests that the cycle existence region expands as the probability of a liquidity shockγ$\gamma$decreases toward zero. In a two-period cycle without mixing, the peak-to-trough ratioμH,2lμH,1l$\frac{\mu ^{l}_{H,2}}{\mu ^{l}_{H,1}}$decreases withγ$\gamma$, which explains why the existence condition is easier to satisfy whenγ$\gamma$is small. Whenγ→0$\gamma \rightarrow 0$, however, equilibrium fluctuations also become smaller: the differenceμH,2l−μH,1l${\mu ^{l}_{H,2}-\mu ^{l}_{H,1}}$converges to zero whenγ→0$\gamma \rightarrow 0$. Without liquidity shocks, the cycle degenerates to the no-trade steady state.
Corollary1suggests that longer cycles are more likely to arise when investors have less frequent rebalancing needs. Housing can be seen as a low-γ$\gamma$asset for which long booms are followed by quick busts. Financial assets such as corporate bonds should instead exhibit high-frequency fluctuations. Other predictions for these two markets are discussed in SectionV.
Proposition2shows that a cycle coexists with a mixed-strategy steady state. If market quality is intermediate, no pure-strategy steady state is sustainable. A constantly high price would drive the effective supply ofH$H$assets to its lowest valueγq$\gamma q$by the quality effect. Ifq≤q¯1$q\le \bar{q}_{1}$, it is too low to sustain a high-price equilibrium. Conversely, the effective supplyq$q$with a constantly low price would be too high to rationalize this offer forq≥q̲1$q\ge \protect\underline{q}_{1}$. Hence, ifq∈[q̲1,q¯1]$q\in [\protect\underline{q}_{1},\bar{q}_{1}]$, a steady-state equilibrium is possible only if buyers mix between offers with a constant probability. In the coexisting cycle, the quality effect instead creates fluctuations between offers over time.
Figure3plots market liquidity when existence condition (CT${C_{T}}$) holds only forT≤3$T \le 3$. Panel3(a)focuses on the steady state. Panel3(b)zooms on the parameter range[q̲1,q¯1]$[\protect\underline{q}_{1},\bar{q}_{1}]$over which cycles can coexist. Market liquidity is zero in a trough, so only peak market liquidity is represented. Panel3(b)suggests that when cycles coexist, the longer the cycle is, the higher peak liquidity is. Intuitively, the average trading probability over time is pinned down by the same underlying market quality. Hence, a longer trough must be associated with a higher level of peak trading.
The next result shows that when several equilibria coexist, the stability criterion selects the cycle when it exists.
Corollary 2.When condition (C1$C_{1}$) holds, there exists at most one stable stationary equilibrium. Forq≤q̲1$q\le \protect\underline{q}_{1}$(q≥q¯1$q\ge \bar{q}_{1}$), the unique stable equilibrium is the illiquid (liquid) steady state. Forq∈(q̲1,q¯1)$q\in (\protect\underline{q}_{1},\bar{q}_{1})$, the unique stable equilibrium—if it exists—is the periodT$T$cycle forq∈(q̲T,q¯T)$q\in (\protect\underline{q}_{T},\bar{q}_{T})$.

The stability criterion selects the unique equilibrium without buyer mixing between offers. Intuitively, if buyers play a pure strategy in equilibrium, the equilibrium offer may still be strictly preferred after a small perturbation of the state variableμHl$\mu ^{l}_{H}$. The proof shows how to construct a transition path following such a perturbation. Convergence can occur by the first period a high offer is made, if any, in the original stationary equilibrium. Intuitively, after a high offer, law of motion (4) shows that the effective supply is reset to its lowest levelγq$\gamma q$, which belongs to the state space of the stationary equilibrium. In the illiquid steady state, a high offer is never made in equilibrium, so convergence occurs in the limit, as illustrated by Panel1(a).
When market qualityq$q$is intermediate, Corollary2justifies the focus on cycles in which buyers do not mix between offers. The results above also imply that there does not exist a stable stationary equilibrium for all values ofq$q$. For instance, when (CT${C_{T}}$) holds forT=1$T=1$only, there is no stable equilibrium whenq∈(q̲1,q¯1)$q\in (\protect\underline{q}_{1},\bar{q}_{1})$.20
In this weak resale effect case, the model makes strong predictions for the dynamics of asset prices. Cyclical fluctuations can arise in opaque markets with resale, even in the absence of shocks to asset fundamentals. SectionVprovides two applications of these results. First, cycles are related to excess price fluctuations in the corporate bond market. Second, I use the model to shed light on speculative price dynamics documented in the housing market and other asset classes. Before this discussion, the next section completes the equilibrium analysis by describing the strong resale effect case.
I now characterize stable stationary equilibria when condition (C1$C_{1}$) does not hold, that is, when the resale effect is strong. This analysis confirms the result in SectionIV.Athat cycles exist only when investors' discount factor is low. Instead, with a strong resale effect, steady-state multiplicity arises.
Proposition 3.If (C1$C_{1}$) does not hold, there is no cycle. The market quality thresholds in Proposition2satisfyq¯1<q̲1$\bar{q}_{1} < \protect\underline{q}_{1}$and


This result is similar to Proposition 2 in Chiu and Koeppl (2016). The unique stable equilibrium is again the liquid (illiquid) steady state when market qualityq$q$is high (low). Whenq$q$is intermediate, however, with a strong resale effect, equilibrium liquidity dynamics are different. First, there is no cycle: when the discount factor is high, liquidity today cannot be different from liquidity tomorrow. Second, for intermediate market qualityq$q$, the liquid steady state now coexists with the illiquid steady state.
Equilibrium multiplicity for intermediate values of market qualityq$q$is due to the resale effect. As explained above, if future buyers offer a high (low) price, current buyers are more likely to offer a high (low) price. When investors are very forward-looking, this intertemporal complementarity leads to equilibrium multiplicity. Formally, the thresholdq̲1$\protect\underline{q}_{1}$below which an illiquid steady state exists is increasing with the discount factorδ$\delta$and eventually exceedsq¯1$\bar{q}_{1}$whenδ$\delta$is high enough. Intuitively, in an illiquid steady state, a buyer who would deviate to make a high offer would pay the present value of information rents. This cost of deviating to a high offer is thus increasing in the discount factorδ$\delta$. Hence, an illiquid steady state can be sustained for higher values ofq$q$. When there is coexistence, the illiquid steady state is inefficient because of the lack of intertemporal coordination between buyers.
In the strong resale effect case, the quality effect does not generate fluctuations, but it still implies a negative relationship between the quality of assets for sale and market liquidity. Consider a value of market qualityq$q$in the interval[q¯1,q̲1]$[\bar{q}_{1},\protect\underline{q}_{1}]$for which steady states coexist. From law of motion (4), the effective supply ofH$H$assets isγq$\gamma q$in the liquid steady state, below the effective supplyq$q$in the illiquid steady state, becauseγ<1$\gamma <1$. High market liquidity implies thatH$H$assets flow toh$h$valuation buyers, who do not resell unless they receive a liquidity shock. The pool of sellers is thus less favorable in the liquid steady state. Prices are mostly determined by the resale effect; however, a high offer is optimal despite the worse pool of assets if assets can be resold at a high price.
article.V. Empirical Implications:
This section derives empirical predictions with applications for two asset classes motivating the analysis: corporate bonds and housing. As already argued, these two markets are reasonable testing grounds for the model because information asymmetry is plausible and trading is decentralized.
These predictions are derived from the novel weak-resale-effect case analyzed in SectionIV.Ain which the equilibrium is a cycle for intermediate values of market quality. In this case, with few ingredients, the model delivers a rich set of predictions. In SectionV.A, I argue that the model provides a liquidity-based explanation for excess price fluctuations in corporate bonds. In SectionV.B, I interpret cycles as repeated speculative episodes.
In an equilibrium cycle, prices fluctuate while asset fundamentals are constant. By construction, the share of high-quality assets in the market is fixed atq$q$, but the composition of assets sold, and thus liquidity, change endogenously over time.
Prediction 1.In opaque asset markets, prices fluctuate more than fundamentals due to time-varying liquidity.

Prediction1offers a liquidity-based rationale for excess price fluctuations, similar to Daley and Green (2015). These fluctuations have been documented for corporate bonds and housing by Bao and Pan (2013) and Glaeser et al. (2014), respectively. An alternative explanation may be that these patterns are generated by the same discount rate fluctuations that explain excess volatility in stocks (see Shiller (1981)). However, this explanation is not supported by Kuvshinov (2020) who finds a weak correlation between the discount rates for equity, housing, and corporate bonds. Consistent with a liquidity-based explanation, Bai, Bali, and Wen (2019) show that liquidity is an important driver of bond prices, which is distinct from traditional stock factors. Their liquidity measure, as well as the bond illiquidity metric inBao, Pan, and Wang (2011), exhibit frequent fluctuations, consistent with Prediction1.
Prediction 2.In a cycle, price changes exhibit reversal, that is,γ>0$\gamma >0$.
The proof of this result is in AppendixA. Although there is short-term momentum in the trough of a cycle of periodT≥3$T\ge 3$, the unconditional price reversal measureγ$\gamma$is negative. This negative sign is driven by the strong reversal around the cycle peak: the last price increase in the troughΔpT$\Delta p_{T}$is the largest and the magnitude of the ensuing price dropΔp1$\Delta p_{1}$is equal to the sum of all price increases in the trough. Prediction2is consistent with the empirical evidence in Bao, Pan, and Wang (2011) for corporate bonds.21Because price reversals are driven by liquidity in the model, the result also corroborates their interpretation ofγ$\gamma$as a proxy for illiquidity. In support of information asymmetry as a driver of illiquidity, Ivaschenko (2020) shows in a recent paper that price reversals are stronger for more opaque bonds.
In this section, I interpret cycles as repeated speculative episodes, also called bubbles. This interpretation is motivated by the observation that the price of anL$L$asset in a cycle trough is driven not by its fundamental value but by its resale value at the peak, as shown by equation (19). Harrison and Kreps (1978) define speculative behavior as a trader's willingness to pay more than the fundamental value of an asset because he can later resell it at a higher price. As the notion of fundamental value is ambiguous in this model, I compare the equilibrium price to a conservative benchmark for the value of theL$L$asset, given byp¯L:=uLh1−δ$\bar{p}_{L}:=\frac{u^{h}_{L}}{1-\delta }$. This value is the discounted utility flow of an asset held by a high-valuation investor in every period.22
Prediction 3.In a cycle trough, theL$L$asset price exceeds the fundamental valuep¯L$\bar{p}_{L}$ifδ>uLh−uLluHl−uLl$\delta > \frac{u^{h}_{L}-u^{l}_{L}}{u^{l}_{H}-u^{l}_{L}}$.

Prediction3follows from comparingp¯L$\bar{p}_{L}$to theL$L$asset price in the last trough period, using equation (19). In the limituLh→uLl$u^{h}_{L}\rightarrow u^{l}_{L}$, the condition in Prediction3is trivial, and thus, it can hold jointly with the existence condition for a cycle (CT${C_{T}}$). Prediction3is intuitive: prices exceed fundamental values if the resale effect generating speculation is strong (highδ$\delta$).
Equilibrium speculation is not a trivial result, even under asymmetry of information. Obviously, anL$L$asset trades at a speculative price at the peak of the cycle because it is pooled with high-quality assets. However, Prediction3shows that speculation can happen during the trough, when onlyL$L$assets are traded, that is, when the offer is separating. The speculative component reflects buyers' ability to later resell a lemon in a good pool at the peak. Such speculation can arise because the trading history is opaque. This prediction is also made by Asriyan, Fuchs, and Green (2019) in a similar environment.
Liquidity cycles may thus contribute to speculative dynamics observed for several assets (see Cutler, Poterba, and Summers (1991)). Glaeser et al. (2014) identify these dynamics in the housing market and, more recently, Enoksen et al. (2020) show that cryptocurrencies exhibit recurring bubble episodes.
As in SectionV.A, I show that the model not only provides a story for the empirical pattern of interest—in this case, price speculation—but also offers stark predictions supported by empirical evidence.
Prediction 4.For a cycle of periodT≥3$T\ge 3$, the rate of price change increases during the boom, that is,Δpt+1≥Δpt$\Delta p_{t+1} \ge \Delta p_{t}$, for2≤t≤T−1$2\le t \le T-1$.

The proof of Prediction2shows that the change in the asset price in the trough of aT$T$-period cycle is given byΔpt=δT−t(uHl−uLl)$\Delta p_{t} = \delta ^{T-t}(u^{l}_{H}-u^{l}_{L})$. Prediction4follows immediately. In a cycle, the price increases whenT−t$T-t$decreases due to the increasing resale value of the asset. This price change also increases because the difference in the expected resale value increases as the peak gets closer. In the model, a speculative episode is thus characterized by acceleration before the bubble bursts. Greenwood, Shleifer, and You (2019) show that acceleration is a typical feature of price rallies that end up in busts.
Finally, many accounts of bubbles describe the asset as a “hot potato” that speculators pass to the next investors. To formalize this narrative, I compute the expected holding time of the asset along the equilibrium cycle. The expected holding time in periodt$t$, denoted asEHTt$EHT_{t}$, is defined as the average expected time before an asset bought in periodt$t$is resold by its owner. The next result shows that this holding time decreases during a boom.
Prediction 5.In a cycle of periodT$T$, the expected holding time of an asset is


The proof of Prediction5is in AppendixA. The intuition for the result is as follows. Consider first the trough during which onlyL$L$assets are traded. At the peak, allL$L$asset owners sell, and thus, the expected holding time of an asset bought in the previous period is equal to one. An investor who buys two periods before the peak must wait for the peak to resell unless he receives a liquidity shock. Hence, the expected holding time is longer. Extending the logic, the expected holding time in the trough increases with the time to the next peak. For an asset bought at the peak, another effect plays a role: at the peak, an asset bought can be of high quality and, even if the new owner then received a liquidity shock, she would only trade at the next peak.
This pattern of expected holding time is consistent with the empirical evidence in Zwick, Nathanson, and DeFusco (2020) that there was a dramatic increase in speculative volume during the 2000/2005 housing boom. Following Bayer et al. (2020), they identify speculative episodes as times when the expected holding period of a house decreases. Prediction5is consistent with this characterization of speculation.
article.VI. Welfare:
Assumption 3.(CT${C_{T}}$) holds only forT=2$T=2$,q∈[q̲1,q¯1]$q\in [\protect\underline{q}_{1},\bar{q}_{1}]$, anduLh=uLl=uL$u^{h}_{L}=u^{l}_{L}=u_{L}$.
The first part of Assumption3implies that only two stationary equilibria exist: a steady state in which buyers make a high offer with probabilityπss$\pi ^{ss}$, and a two-period cycle with liquidityπ0(2)∈(0,1]$\pi _{0}^{(2)}\in (0,1]$at the peak andπ1(2)=0$\pi _{1}^{(2)}=0$in the trough. Focusing on two-period cycles helps deliver the intuition about the welfare properties of cycles. The second part of Assumption3implies there is no gain from trading theL$L$asset. This part of the assumption helps obtain closed-form solutions for equilibrium market liquidity, but has a limited impact on the welfare analysis because gains from trading theL$L$asset are always realized.
Proposition 4.There exists a threshold for market qualityq̂∈(q̲2,q¯1)$\hat{q}\in (\protect\underline{q}_{2},\bar{q}_{1})$such that the cycle delivers higher welfare if and only ifq≤q̂$q\le \hat{q}$.
Proposition4states that forq∈[q̂,q¯1]$q\in [\hat{q},\bar{q}_{1}]$, the stable two-period cycle is less efficient than the unstable steady state. InInternet AppendixIA.IV, I show that a countercyclical policy with subsidies and taxes can then increase welfare. For market qualityq∈[q̲2,q̂]$q\in [\protect\underline{q}_{2},\hat{q}]$, the cycle is more efficient.
I now explain the welfare comparison in Proposition4, focusing first on the region[q̲1,q̲2]$[\protect\underline{q}_{1},\protect\underline{q}_{2}]$. Recall that the market is liquid in periodt$t$ifΓt≥0$\Gamma _{t}\ge 0$withΓt$\Gamma _{t}$the net benefit of a high offer. In the steady state, this condition holds as an equality. In every period, there is just enough liquidity such that, given the quality effect, a high offer is again weakly profitable the next period. In the cycle, liquidity is higher at the peak compared to the steady state, that is,π0(2)>πss$\pi ^{(2)}_{0}>\pi ^{ss}$, but this gain is not fully offset by lower liquidity in the trough. The welfare gain of a cycle over the steady state can thus be measured by the difference between equilibrium market liquidity at the peak,π0(2)$\pi _{0}^{(2)}$, and the lower amountπ∼0(2)$\tilde{\pi }_{0}^{(2)}$that would be necessary to also sustain liquidity in the trough. Panel5(b)of Figure5plots this difference as a function ofq$q$. The hump-shaped pattern of this liquidity benefit reflects the U-shaped pattern of the (negative) net benefit from trading in the troughΓ1$\Gamma _{1}$in Panel5(a).
Due to this liquidity benefit, the cycle dominates the steady state whenq∈[q̲1,q̲2]$q\in [\protect\underline{q}_{1},\protect\underline{q}_{2}]$. Whenq∈[q̲2,q¯1]$q\in [\protect\underline{q}_{2},\bar{q}_{1}]$, however, there is a liquidity cost of the cycle relative to the steady state. In this region, buyers make a strictly positive profit at the cycle peak, that is,Γ0>0$\Gamma _{0}>0$, as illustrated in Panel5(a), but this profit is earned at the expense of liquidity in the trough. To see this, defineπ∼1(2)>0$\tilde{\pi }_{1}^{(2)}>0$as the maximum amount of trading in the trough compatible with buyers trading at the peak.24Increasing trading in the trough from its equilibrium valueπ1(2)=0$\pi _{1}^{(2)}=0$toπ∼1(2)$\tilde{\pi }_{1}^{(2)}$has a clear benefit with no social cost at the peak. It would reduce the buyers' profit but the market would still be liquid, by definition ofπ∼1(2)$\tilde{\pi }_{1}^{(2)}$. Hence, the variableπ∼1(2)$\tilde{\pi }_{1}^{(2)}$, plotted in Panel5(b), measures the liquidity cost of a cycle compared to the steady state. This cost is strictly positive only in the region[q̲2,q¯1]$ [\protect\underline{q}_{2},\bar{q}_{1}]$in which buyers make a strictly positive profit. When the liquidity cost of a cycle exceeds the benefit described above, the steady state dominates.
The result that the cycle is less efficient when market qualityq$q$is close to the thresholdq¯1$\bar{q}_{1}$for a liquid steady state is not surprising. In the limit asq$q$approachesq¯1$\bar{q}_{1}$, there is no steady-state misallocation because market liquidityπss$\pi ^{ss}$converges toward one. In a cycle, however, every second period, a small massγq$\gamma q$oflH$lH$investors do not trade: misallocation persists even whenq$q$is very close toq¯1$\bar{q}_{1}$. Unlike in the steady state, an increase in market quality translates into a higher buyer's profit at the peak but average liquidity does not change.
Overall, the welfare analysis shows that although investors are risk-neutral, fluctuations matter from a welfare point of view. Welfare in the (stable) cyclical equilibrium can be higher than in the (unstable) steady-state equilibrium. This result arises because cyclical liquidity is high precisely when the effective supply ofH$H$assets is high, which reduces misallocation.
article.VII. Conclusion:
I present a model to characterize the liquidity dynamics of a secondary market in which asset quality is heterogeneous and trade history is opaque. With few plausible ingredients, the model generates rich dynamics. When market quality is intermediate, endogenous liquidity cycles can emerge in the absence of changes to fundamentals. Cycles arise because of a quality effect whereby past trading intensity has a negative effect on current market liquidity. Price and volume dynamics are shown to be speculative due to a resale effect. These fluctuations can be efficient compared to a steady-state equilibrium with a constant level of trade. The model also shows that when investors are more patient, cycles disappear but steady states with different degrees of liquidity coexist. The findings are useful to rationalize empirical patterns for several asset classes, including corporate bonds.
The analysis leaves several avenues for future research. While the supply of assets is fixed and constant in my model, primary market issuance could react to secondary market liquidity. Moreover, incentives to produce high-quality assets may change along the cycle. Finally, it would be interesting to investigate how policies to increase transparency or efforts to migrate OTC trading toward centralized markets would affect the equilibrium in this environment.
